content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 25, 1991

Robert M. Hertzberg
MARGOLIS, HERTZBERG AND MORIN
3550 Wilshire Boulevard, Suite 1418
Los Angeles, CA  90010-2415

Re:  Your Requests for Advice
Our File No. A-91-319

Dear Mr. Hertzberg:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you pursuant to the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹  This advice was requested on behalf of Assemblymember
Xavier Becerra and candidate Mike Hernandez concerning travel
provided to Assemblymember Becerra by Mr. Hernandez.

This letter confirms that your letter dated June 20, 1991,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on
June 19, 1991.  You stated in that telephone conversation that
Mr. Hernandez had advanced to a special runoff election in his
campaign for a city council seat in the City of Los Angeles.  You
also stated that since the Assemblymember was a supporter of
Mr. Hernandez, Mr. Hernandez paid with campaign funds to have the
Assemblymember fly to Los Angeles to participate in his victory
celebration.  You asked whether the Assemblymember was required to
reimburse Mr. Hernandez for the payment pursuant to Regulation
18726.1 (copy enclosed).

I advised that the Assemblymember need not reimburse Mr.
Hernandez for the payment for his travel.  Regulation 18727.5
provides in a pertinent part:

(a)  Travel costs and reimbursements therefor
paid by a "committee" as defined by Government Code

__________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-319
Page 2


     Section 82013(a), including a "controlled
committee," shall be valued as follows:

     (1) A reimbursement, advance, or other
payment for actual travel expenses (including
meals and lodging) or for other actual and
legitimate campaign expenses made by such a
committee or controlled committee is neither
income nor a gift to an official and need not
be disclosed on the official's Statement of
Economic Interests if the expenditures are
reported by the committee in accordance with
the provisions of Government Code Sections
84100, et seq.

     Thus, so long as Mr. Hernandez's campaign committee reports
the expenditure, the Assemblymember has not received a reportable
gift subject to any of the Act's limits. (See generally, Section
89504.) Moreover, the Assemblymember would not have received a
reportable contribution under your facts as well. (See generally,
Raye Advice Letter, No. A-83-162; Rogers Advice Letter, No. A-80-
08-046, copies enclosed.)

     Finally, as we discussed, such an expenditure by
Mr. Hernandez's campaign committee is permitted so long as the
expenditure is directly related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose. (Section 89513(f).) Since the payment was
provided to the Assemblymember so that the Assemblymember could
appear at Mr. Hernandez's campaign event, and the event was held
in proximity to an election for which Mr. Hernandez will be
campaigning, the expenditure appears to be directly related to a
political purpose.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   By: John W. Wallace
                                       Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosures
